quotes.quote: In the first 4-8 weeks there is often rapid progress. Enthusiasm is very high and forecasting of potential speeds that one may be able to reach are being dreamed about. Gaining speed is easy!
In this initial 3-6 month period it is not uncommon for golfers to see 5-10mph increase with driver club head speed. Just from practicing swinging faster. This is game changing for many golfers

After this period many golfers report a plateau, and it seems like further progress cannot be made even though they remain consistent with their training. This generally occurs somewhere approximately 6 months into training

For example, 10 swings at maximum speed 2 times a week might be enough of a stimulus for a beginner to see improvements in speed. For someone with a lot of training experience under their belt, this amount of stress will likely not be a big enough stimulus to trigger further improvement.
someone 6 months into speed training may need 30-50 swings at maximum speed two or three times a week to see improvement

Nearly finished 2nd sessions with Stack. But disappointingly stalled on 102mph for the last 2 months. Do seem to have been making better progress with just a couple of range sessions with the driver per week. Or maybe I was close to my ceiling anyway when I started Stack in December. It certainly appears it has either given me nothing, or is the cause of my stalling.
Still 5mph faster than I was last summer. Expect it to be worth a couple of shots improvement on the hc.
Training Session 1 - 12/17/22: Green 118, Blue 113, Red 107. Training Session 136 - 4/1/24: Green 131, Blue 126, Red 121. PR's: Green 133, Blue 127, Red 121

Not really sure exactly what my starting driver speed was (while hitting a ball), but I think it was about 104. These days a average 112-113 at the end of a speed session. Last spring/summer I was at about 110 on course and I think I'm 1-2 mph faster this year.

One thing I learned last year was that you really do have to be careful with how you schedule your training and workouts. Last spring I actually got slower bc I wasn't giving myself enough rest after a gym session. I've seen much better results in the last 8 months if I go in to a speed training session with a day of rest from my lifts.
quotes.results_mentioned: got slower due to poor scheduling

quotes.quote: I would say that everyone will see an increase in swing speed through just doing speed training. What it actually does is train your central nervous system to move faster and doesn't have anything to do with getting stronger or more flexible. Which is why you can see results REALLY quickly but it take time to make those changes more permanent.

For all you guys using speed training tools, does it readily convert to the course? I'm 59, fairly fit and I know can swing faster than I do on the tee, proven by testing inside with GC Quad, but when I stand on the tee with the risk of a bad outcome, I am more focussed and feel I swing at maybe 85-90%.
quotes.results_mentioned: swings 85-90% on course vs max in training

quotes.quote: There's absolutely no doubt that it translates to the course. I don't feel like I'm swinging any harder than I did 16 months ago but now my 85% swing goes 20yds further. Combine that with the fact that I'm 1 club longer with my irons I'm now hitting 3 clubs less into greens on my home course than I did before I started speed training.

For example, on a 430yd hole before I might have 155 in and be hitting an 7 iron in, now I have 135 in and hitting a PW. I'd rather have PW from the rough every day over 7 iron from the middle of the fairway.

It most certainly does. Doubt I swing on the course, with driver at least, at the speed I push at the range. Nevertheless, putting drives out distances I had never experienced before. 2 clubs lower into greens. Its a very striking experience to see the same golf course but in a very different light effectively.
But has it made a difference to your scoring? Distance is fine but I've yet to see it on a scorecard

My career low happened last season and my handicap dropped 3 strokes. I did not practice any more than I did before and I got no lessons. So yeah... and i'm 1-2mph faster this year so I'll let you know more later!
Anyone else do the Stack? I've also stalled.. on about 101 mph but still on the Foundation Course. I don't do any swing training or weights.. I guess that is essential to increase speed.. doh.
Progress is never linear, there's always peaks and troughs. Keep going and at some point you'll breakthrough that barrier and move onwards and upwards. I've seen this with my own training.

I obviously don't do stack, but I definitely saw my progress slow to a crawl last year about 3 months into the program. What I have noticed in the time since then is that I have become extremely consistent. My numbers are almost ALWAYS within 1mph of each other on every drill from session to session and week to week.
Absolutely has made a difference to my scorecard. Over the winter I had 3 rounds in low 80s and also shot a 79 at my home course for the first time ever - my index is 14.2 currently.

I started out 14 weeks ago with an average club head speed of 93 miles an hour and an average carry distance of 220 yards... after six weeks... I've got my swing speed up to 100 miles an hour and my carry distance up to 241
but i've hit a bit of a plateau and i need to break through it

my clubhead speed is up to 105.1 miles an hour on average ball speed is up to 153.5 miles per hour smash factor 1.45 which has stayed really consistent all the way through this whole experiment but you'll see my carry distance on average is now up to 261.2 yards obviously i'm so pleased that's 41 yards extra on average carry distance

what i see with this on the course is just it's changed my game whereas before you might have been struggling to reach your bunker or you'd be roll out would be into the bunkers you'd be trying to plot a way around it you're now able to hit it over it you get so many more birdie chances with that bit of extra distance

The theory is that you use their technique to raise your top speed, and that raises whatever percentage you're comfortable swinging at. They've got some videos on their web site with the protocols and how long you're supposed to do them. Two weeks isn't nearly long enough for any tangible results.

most people will gain raw swing speed if they stick with the program for an extended amount of time. Now whether or not that translates to the course depends on if you can 'control' that increase or not. If you gain 5mph swing speed but your smash factor drops, then you may not see much of a difference in overall distance.
quotes.results_mentioned: speed gain may not translate if smash factor drops

quotes.quote: I have seen a few people increase their swing speed by quite a bit, but they also opened up a wild 2 way miss. My personal experience with the people I know that have used swing speed training devices is that the lower to moderate swing speed people tend to see more 'results' on the course than the people who can already swing it 110+ and just want more.
quotes.results_mentioned: wild 2-way miss, better results for slower swingers

quotes.quote: One of the older guys(68 years old) I play with was swinging in the high 80s-low 90s when he started. Now he swings in the high 90's and he has gained quite a bit of useful length.

Another guy I know went from a 105mph swing speed to around 115mph now, but his drives are so much more erratic and inconsistent than before.......but ever once in a while he gets a hold of one and brags how he had never hit it to that spot before

I used them for a while and there's no question they work for getting more speed. I didn't even do a good job following the program & they still helped a lot. I also like swinging the heavy one slowly to warm up/stretch before a round.

Accuracy was an issue for me at first. The extra speed opened up the big left miss in a way I'd never had to worry about before. You have to be willing to put in range work to adjust your sequencing to handle the new speed. I needed to stop trying so hard to turn the club over because my hands got way faster.

I bought a set and the SS device to see what they were about. Quickly got increased CH speed, both at max effort and regular cruising speed. What I wasn't expecting was I guess what I will call a mental and swing improvement benefit - I notice it has helped me swing more free through the bag; I also notice it has forced me to improve my through swing sequence and thus my consistency.
quotes.results_mentioned: mental and swing improvement, better sequencing

quotes.quote: Disclaimer: I bought the sticks and the radar, and followed the protocols religiously for almost a year. I never got hurt, but I never saw any swing speed gains, either, but I don't think there was any negative carryover to my 'real' swing from trying to learn to swing faster.
quotes.results_mentioned: no swing speed gains after nearly 1 year

quotes.quote: I eventually got more benefits from doing a LOT of mobility work, especially on my hips, and using slam balls, kettlebell swings, and other golf-specific workout stuff.
quotes.results_mentioned: mobility work and fitness more effective

quotes.quote: I gained 10 mph in clubhead speed and lost 20-30 yards on my average drive due quality of contact and accuracy issues. A theory that I've heard, which I tend to agree with, is that speed training helps the low handicappers who already have excellent mechanics add speed. It also helps high handicappers who need to swing faster and more athletically in general. For mid-handicappers like myself, the swing sticks can magnify swing flaws
My SS increased by 5mph (2 1/2 months) but I bought the Christmas deal with counterweight one. I think the counter weight one helped me the most.

Unless you are able to hit balls several times a week, I just think it wreaks havoc on your swing. Took about a month break due to an elbow issue. During that month I struck the ball better than the prior month and a half when I was speed training.
quotes.results_mentioned: struck ball better after stopping speed training

quotes.quote: started back on the sticks this week...blue stick only method. Hit balls today and could barely find the face. I was dancing around the ball...all kinds of movement going on. Just don't think it's good unless you can hit balls often...probably right after your speed session.
quotes.results_mentioned: could barely find the face, erratic movement

quotes.quote: I use the superspeed with the MLM2PRO and have went from 98-99 MPH to averaging 105-106. I do the dynamic and level 2 warm ups before each range session or round of golf. As others have mentioned I have been able to consistently maintain the same swing speed throughout the round.

I've been a student of Mike's for roughly 6 months and in that period went from being ranked 96th to 47th. I've seen my speed increase from 143mph to 153mph. If you are serious about creating more distance, then you need to speed train!

I found out that there was still some giddy up in this old body if I followed his program. In one week, I have improved CHS an average of 5MPH and truly believe if I follow the Mach3 Speed program there is at least another 5MPH to be gained. I'm a believer!

On March 3, 2018, I made it into Mike's gym and began my Mach3 training. That day I averaged 106mph with a top speed of 109. I stuck with training 2-3 days per week, and on July 3 I averaged 114 and hit my top speed of 121.

I've now been running the Mach3 programme for my clients and England U18 squad since September 2018. The awesome thing about this type of training is that it not only improves speed but develops great timing and consistency too.
my clubhead speed is up 6-8 mph through the bag. I'm hitting it farther at 68 than I did at 30, 40, and 50 years of age.
quotes.results_mentioned: +6-8mph through the bag, farther at 68 than younger ages

quotes.quote: I think speed training is, in general, 'low hanging fruit' for guys that are already strong enough, flexible enough, mobile enough, and have really solid technique, and just need to recruit and activate some fast-twitch capability. But the number of guys who fit that bill is FAR exceeded by the number of guys that are deficient in one or more of those categories
the number of guys that are deficient in one or more of those categories, and for them, just trying to swing faster is a GREAT recipe for frustration (best case) or injury (worst case)

There is a lot of data from golf and other sports that shows pretty clearly that the number of swings in the SSG protocols is way over the number needed to make improvement even if improvement is possible.
quotes.results_mentioned: SSG protocols may have excessive volume

quotes.quote: There is also a lot of data that indicates that swinging a stick with weight similar to your own driver (that's the blue stick in the SSG group) is where the gains really are, rather than the three stick method.
I'm like 16 months into consistent speed training and I'm still trying to figure out what I'm doing different on days I'm at my fastest. I take even take notes on those days to try to replicate it.

I also feel like I am able to hit my top speeds earlier in the session. Up until maybe 6 months ago my first set of green swings was always up to 5 mph slower than the finishing set of green. Now I'm almost always within 1 mph of my max on my very first set.
quotes.results_mentioned: faster warmup, within 1mph of max immediately

quotes.quote: I've personally built up my swing speed from 125mph- 144mph in 6 months
Swinging hard 3 days a week makes you want to swing hard on the course. 1st 9 holes Saturday concerned me because I was swinging hard at everything.
quotes.results_mentioned: tendency to swing too hard on course

quotes.quote: Hit a new PB tonight on my baseline test tonight, hitting a single 116mph swing. Also measured a shot at my course on the weekend, total was 250 and that was with no rollout, just carry.
Also, speed training does absolute wonders for your weight shift, transition, and sequencing.
I have recurrence of an old wrist injury which has just hampered my training this past week - actually injured it carrying my DJ equipment which is rather annoying.
